`ShowFile` in `internal/gitutil/git.go` classified a git failure as *file-absent* (returning `("", false, nil)`) whenever the error text contained `"Path"`, `"does not exist"`, `"not found"`, or `"exists on disk, but not in"`. But `run()` wraps errors as `git show REV:PATH: <stderr>`, so the path (argv) is **always** part of the error string. Any real error — bad rev, IO failure, context cancellation — on a path that happens to contain the substring `"Path"` (e.g. `src/PathHelper.go`) was silently swallowed as a missing file.

## Fix

Classify against git's **stderr only**, never the wrapped error that echoes the argv. Added a `runWithStderr` helper that returns stdout and stderr separately; `run()` now delegates to it and keeps its existing signature, so no other callers change. The redundant `"Path"` clause is gone (git's real capital-P message is already caught by `"exists on disk, but not in"`).

- Genuinely missing paths at a valid rev still return `("", false, nil)`.
- Real failures (including bad rev on a `"Path"` file) now propagate as a non-nil error.

Fixes #31

## Test evidence

Added `TestShowFileClassifiesErrorsByStderrNotPath` (in `internal/gitutil/git_test.go`), which commits `src/PathHelper.go` and asserts:
- `ShowFile(ctx, repo, "BADREV", "src/PathHelper.go")` returns a non-nil error (regression).
- A genuinely missing path at `HEAD` returns `("", false, nil)`.
- An existing file at `HEAD` returns its content, `ok=true`, `nil`.

Verification (all pass):
```
gofmt -s -w . ; gofmt -l internal   # empty
go vet ./...                          # ok
go build ./...                        # ok
go test -race ./internal/gitutil/     # ok
```","state":null,"user":null,"assignees":null,"labels":null,"milestone":null,"state_reason":null,"comments":null,"created_at":null,"updated_at":null,"closed_at":null,"display_url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/36","display_title":"fix(gitutil): stop misclassifying git errors as file-absent"},{"url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/43","title":"Improve agent retrieval and focused graph navigation","issue_number":43,"body":"<!-- entire-trail-link-start -->
https://entire.io/gh/entireio/entire-graph/trails/1
<!-- entire-trail-link-end -->

## What changed

- Improves agent search recall with adaptive syntax preselection, sparse lexical regions, reciprocal-rank fusion, focused source windows, and conservative authored/generated mirror deduplication.
- Adds `entire graph neighbors` for bounded callers, callees, constructor edges, and compact exact two-hop path families.
- Completes general Go, TypeScript/JavaScript, and Python call resolution gaps exposed by repository-disjoint development diagnostics.
- Adds `entire graph index`, a durable query-independent HEAD preindex with exact commit/tree/profile/provider provenance, cache-hit telemetry, and repeatable ignore/include scopes.
- Adds an exact-commit Git preselection path that reuses the full prepared graph directly, preserves cold/cache parity, and reports honest Git-scan versus Go-hydration telemetry.
- Adds source-faithful proto2 compatibility for omitted syntax declarations, legacy field labels, and groups while keeping malformed files fail-visible.
- Lets committed search and neighbor calls prove they consumed the prepared cache; agent output reports the cache state directly.
- Keeps positive agent byte budgets bounded and removes generated mirrors from both semantic and deep sparse rankings without merging sibling monorepo packages.

There are no repository-, benchmark-, qrel-, rank-, score-, or outcome-specific product branches.

## Validation

- Exact head: `4f4cf4fca7f847b5560e095328232cdf5134b507`.
- Exact staged runtime SHA-256: `e0e2738206d2e4d52fddf509584bd9cecc253f9b349dee599281012ca1c75ed5`; Go build metadata reports the same revision and `vcs.modified=false`.
- `mise run check` passes on the exact head: format, vet, build, and `go test -race ./...`.
- All seven Linux/macOS/Windows GitHub checks pass on the exact head.
- Local adversarial review identified cache/default, parity, budget, mirror-dedup, Unicode preselection, provenance, and telemetry defects; the follow-up commits fix them with regression coverage.
- The Windows-only committed-grep test failure was a portability defect in its colon-containing test filename (an NTFS alternate data stream, not a tracked file). The portable regression now passes without skipping Windows coverage.
- On the pinned gin full-profile corpus, valid proto2 support changes completeness from degraded to OK, removes the sole parse failure, and recovers three semantic symbols plus three `DEFINES` relations.
- The earlier Bugbot focused-region-test issue is already fixed: both tests are independent registered Go tests and execute in the full suite.

Deterministic replay on the three frozen predefined development repositories, with setup excluded and a fresh cache format:

| Repository | Cold full setup | Cached search | Cached neighbors | Useful neighborhood |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| chi | 0.854s | 24ms | 33ms | 1 caller × 6 callees |
| Zod | 5.418s | 62ms | 63ms | 7 callers × 2 callees |
| Click | 3.090s | 57ms | 94ms | 4 callers × 6 callees |

Every timed retrieval above reported `Index: cache-hit`. Zod includes the real abstract `_parse` target; Click includes the real `_AtomicFile` constructor. Those semantics must be frozen prospectively in any fresh evaluation contract.

An additional unpaid cache preflight on the newly frozen, repository-disjoint TypeScript/PHP/Java development slice completed full setup with zero partial failures. Reopening the exact caches reported hits in 33–88ms; synthetic warm validation searches completed internally in 76–189ms. Setup remains outside task timing.

## Evidence boundary

The previous paid three-repository smoke remains immutable diagnostic evidence, but its wall-time comparison is invalid: Graph and CBM indexing occurred inside the agent timer, Graph was forced through asymmetric duplicate work, CBM was degraded, and attempts competed concurrently. It is not rescored or rewritten here.

No new paid agentic run is included. A broad superiority claim still requires a fresh outcome-blind predefined slice, frozen comparator/product heads, repeated paired attempts, and the corrected preindexed protocol in Brain-Bench PR #34.

## Scope

No Entire Brain changes, V7/V8 catalog/ranking/scoring/publication authorization changes, or benchmark-specific product behavior.

structuredContent.body:
## Summary

Fixes #33.

`internal/sem/parser.go`'s TypeScript static-accessor mask matched one-or-more whitespace (`\s+`) between `static` and `accessor`, but the replacement hardcoded a single space:

```go
tsStaticAccessorMethodPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`\bstatic\s+accessor(\s*\()`)
// replacement: "static accessoR${1}"
```

So `static  accessor(` (two spaces) was rewritten to `static accessoR(` — one byte shorter. The parser parses the masked bytes but extracts entity text from the **original** source using masked-tree offsets, so any length change shifts every subsequent offset and corrupts each following entity's name/signature/body-hash. (Fires only for a static method literally named `accessor`, i.e. `accessor` followed by `(`.)

## Fix

Length-preserving rewrite: capture everything except the final `r` of `accessor` and rewrite only that char to `R`, preserving all matched bytes (and whitespace) verbatim — matching the sibling masks:

```go
tsStaticAccessorMethodPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`(\bstatic\s+accesso)r(\s*\()`)
// replacement: "${1}R${2}"
```

## Test evidence

Added regression tests in `internal/sem/parser_test.go`:

- `TestMaskTypeScriptStaticAccessorMethodPreservesLength` — asserts `len(output) == len(input)` for one-space and multi-space inputs.
- `TestTreeSitterParserTypeScriptStaticAccessorMaskPreservesFollowingEntity` — parses `static  accessor()` (two spaces) followed by `bar()`; asserts the following method is `C.bar` with the same name/signature/body-hash as the one-space variant.

Confirmed both tests **fail before** the fix (the following entity corrupts to `C.ba` / signature offset drift) and **pass after**.

Verification (all pass):

```
gofmt -l internal        # clean
go vet ./...             # ok
go build ./...           # ok
go test ./internal/sem/  # ok
go test -race -run 'TestMaskTypeScriptStaticAccessorMethodPreservesLength|TestTreeSitterParserTypeScriptStaticAccessorMaskPreservesFollowingEntity' ./internal/sem/  # ok
```

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

---

## Follow-up: second mask of the same class (found during review)

An adversarial review of this fix audited every literal-replacement mask in `parser.go` and found the **same length-changing bug** in `javaModuleImportPattern`:

```go
// pattern ^(\s*import\s+)module\s+  with a fixed 7-space replacement
```

`import module   java.base;` (multiple spaces after `module`, greedy `\s+`) shrank the line and corrupted every downstream entity's offsets (e.g. `class C` → entity `s`, `C.bar` → `s.t b`) — verified empirically via `ParseWithStatus`. Fixed the same way (capture the trailing whitespace, emit a 6-char `module` mask + verbatim whitespace), so it is length-invariant for any whitespace count.

Added tests: `TestMaskJavaModuleImportPreservesLength` and `TestTreeSitterParserJavaModuleImportMaskPreservesFollowingEntity`, plus extended the static-accessor length test with tab/mixed-whitespace inputs and a negative-match test.

Full `go test ./...` passes.

Fixes #41 (the Java `import module` mask, same bug class, found and fixed during this review).

structuredContent.body:
## Summary

`AnalyzeGitRange` (and therefore `AnalyzeCheckpoint`, which delegates to it) called `parser.Parse`, discarding the `ParseStatus` returned by `ParseWithStatus`. When a changed file failed to parse (`E_PARSE_ERROR` / `E_PARSE_TIMEOUT`), the parser returned zero entities with no signal, so `compareEntities(before, nil)` reported **every** entity as a phantom `removed` change (or `added`, if the base side failed) with nothing appended to `result.Warnings`. This violated the guarantee that unparseable files surface as machine-readable partial failures, never silent drops.

## Fix

- Switch both base and head parsing in `AnalyzeGitRange` to `TreeSitterParser{}.ParseWithStatus`.
- When **either** side reports `ParseError` for a file, append a `ProviderWarning` (reusing the existing `Result.Warnings` channel, no schema change) and skip the delta for that file instead of emitting phantom changes. The warning mirrors the provider path's `parseStatus.ParseError → PartialFailure` mapping in `provider.go` (code, severity, effect-on-completeness wording, detail).
- Distinguish "parse failed" (`ParseError` true) from a legitimately emptied/zero-entity file (`ParseError` false): only the former is suppressed+warned; a validly-emptied file still reports its real `removed` changes.
- `AnalyzeCheckpoint` is covered automatically since it delegates to `AnalyzeGitRange`.

`writeText` already ignores `Warnings` (including the pre-existing move-ambiguity warnings), so text output is unchanged; `--json` serializes them via the existing `warnings` tag.

Fixes #30

## Test evidence

New regression test `TestAnalyzeGitRangeSurfacesParseFailures` (end-to-end via a temp git repo + `AnalyzeGitRange`) covers all three cases:
- base valid / head unparseable -> partial-failure warning, no phantom `removed` for alpha/beta.
- base unparseable / head valid -> warning, no phantom `added`.
- genuinely emptied file (valid, zero entities) -> real `removed` changes, no warning.

Verification (all pass):
- `gofmt -l internal` -> empty
- `go vet ./...`
- `go build ./...`
- `go test ./internal/sem/`
- `go test -race -run TestAnalyzeGitRangeSurfacesParseFailures ./internal/sem/`

structuredContent.body:
## Summary

`entire graph search` panicked with `slice bounds out of range [-1:]` and
crashed whenever a graph-expansion candidate's target symbol had a `StartLine`
beyond the end of the file as read during search.

`expandGraphCandidates` (`internal/sem/search.go`) clamped the target region
with `clampRegion`, which returns `(0,0)` when `symbol.StartLine > len(lines)`.
Unlike its sibling builder `makeSearchCandidate`, it had no `start == 0` bailout,
so `strings.Join(lines[snippetStart-1:snippetEnd], "\n")` evaluated as
`lines[-1:0]` and panicked, taking the whole search command down.

## Fix

Add the same guard `makeSearchCandidate` already uses, right after the
`clampRegion` call: when `start == 0`, `continue` and skip that expansion
candidate. Minimal, style-matched, no behavior change for in-range symbols.

## Test evidence

Adds two regression tests in `internal/sem/search_test.go` driving
`expandGraphCandidates` directly:

- `REDACTED` — a `CALLS` relation
  (confidence 1.0) to a target whose `StartLine=100` against a 3-line file. On
  the pre-fix code this test panics with `slice bounds out of range [-1:]`
  (verified by temporarily removing the guard); with the fix it returns cleanly
  and omits the out-of-range candidate.
- `TestExpandGraphCandidatesIncludesInRangeSymbol` — an in-range target still
  yields a candidate with a non-empty snippet.

Verification (all pass):
- `gofmt -s -w .` then `gofmt -l internal` (empty)
- `go vet ./...`
- `go build ./...`
- `go test ./internal/sem/`
- `go test -race -run 'TestExpandGraphCandidates...' ./internal/sem/`

Fixes #32

{"url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/36","number":36,"state":"open","merged":false,"mergeable":true,"draft":false,"body":"## Summary

`ShowFile` in `internal/gitutil/git.go` classified a git failure as *file-absent* (returning `("", false, nil)`) whenever the error text contained `"Path"`, `"does not exist"`, `"not found"`, or `"exists on disk, but not in"`. But `run()` wraps errors as `git show REV:PATH: <stderr>`, so the path (argv) is **always** part of the error string. Any real error — bad rev, IO failure, context cancellation — on a path that happens to contain the substring `"Path"` (e.g. `src/PathHelper.go`) was silently swallowed as a missing file.

## Fix

Classify against git's **stderr only**, never the wrapped error that echoes the argv. Added a `runWithStderr` helper that returns stdout and stderr separately; `run()` now delegates to it and keeps its existing signature, so no other callers change. The redundant `"Path"` clause is gone (git's real capital-P message is already caught by `"exists on disk, but not in"`).

- Genuinely missing paths at a valid rev still return `("", false, nil)`.
- Real failures (including bad rev on a `"Path"` file) now propagate as a non-nil error.

Fixes #31

## Test evidence

Added `TestShowFileClassifiesErrorsByStderrNotPath` (in `internal/gitutil/git_test.go`), which commits `src/PathHelper.go` and asserts:
- `ShowFile(ctx, repo, "BADREV", "src/PathHelper.go")` returns a non-nil error (regression).
- A genuinely missing path at `HEAD` returns `("", false, nil)`.
- An existing file at `HEAD` returns its content, `ok=true`, `nil`.

PR 37
{"url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/37","number":37,"state":"open","merged":false,"mergeable":false,"draft":false,"body":"## Summary

`compareEntities` (internal/sem/analyze.go) keyed entities by `Kind:Name`, so two same-name, same-`Kind` overloads (reachable in C#/Java — not TypeScript) collided in `beforeByKey`/`afterByKey` under last-write-wins: only the last one in file order survived. As a result, editing the **first** overload's signature vanished from the diff (only the enclosing class showed `body_changed`); editing the second worked only coincidentally.

## Fix

Two-phase matching in `keyedEntityMaps` (which replaces the per-side `keyedEntities`; it takes both entity slices at once since matching needs both sides):

1. **Phase 1 — exact pairing.** Entities that share an exact `Kind:Name:Signature` across sides pair up first. True duplicates (an identical `Kind:Name:Signature` appearing multiple times on one side) are disambiguated by occurrence index in file order, so the Nth duplicate on each side pairs with the Nth on the other. This makes mid-list overload insertions, removals, and reorders inert for the untouched overloads instead of cascading phantom `signature_changed` events down the list.
2. **Phase 2 — positional fallback for leftovers.** Entities whose exact signature has no counterpart on the other side get positional ordinals per `Kind:Name` in file order. An in-place signature edit of one overload leaves exactly one leftover with that name on each side, so it still pairs and surfaces as `signature_changed` rather than remove+add.

Notes:

- We deliberately do **not** use a naive pure-signature key (signature always in the key, no positional fallback): that would regress the primary #35 repro (`F(int)`→`F(long)` with `F(string)` present) back to removed+added, because the rename reconciler's signature similarity (~0.33) is far below the 0.92 threshold.
- Phase-1 and phase-2 keys use distinct prefixes (`=` / `~`) so they can never collide.
- A lone, non-overloaded entity still pairs exactly as before (exact match when unchanged, sole leftover when edited), preserving prior single-entity behavior.
- Removed/added (present on only one side) still work: unmatched keys fall through to the existing deleted/added handling and rename reconciliation, which treat keys as opaque strings — keys are ephemeral and never persisted or emitted.

## Test evidence

Nine direct `compareEntities` regression tests (package `sem`, hand-constructed `[]Entity`, no grammar needed):

- `TestCompareEntitiesDisambiguatesSameNameOverloads` — #35 repro/regression guard: editing the FIRST of two `method C.F` overloads (`F(int)`→`F(long)`, `F(string)` untouched) yields exactly one `signature_changed` for the first overload, with no spurious remove/add of the second.
- `TestCompareEntitiesDetectsSecondOverloadEdit` — control: editing only the SECOND overload is reported correctly.
- `TestCompareEntitiesSingleEntityUnchangedBehavior` — regression: a single non-overloaded entity is still reported as before.
- `TestCompareEntitiesAddedOverloadReportedAsAdded` — tail-appended overload surfaces as exactly one `added`.
- `TestCompareEntitiesRemovedOverloadReported` — tail-removed overload surfaces as exactly one `removed`.
- `TestCompareEntitiesTrueDuplicatesEditOne` — identical `Kind:Name:Signature` duplicates; editing one body yields exactly one `body_changed` (occurrence-index pairing).
- `TestCompareEntitiesRemoveFirstOverloadNoCascade` — removing the FIRST of three overloads yields exactly one `removed F(int)` and no phantom `signature_changed` cascade (previously misattributed under pure-ordinal keying).
- `TestCompareEntitiesMidListInsertOnlyAdded` — mid-list insert yields exactly one `added`, nothing else.
- `TestCompareEntitiesReorderedOverloadsNoChanges` — pure reorder of identical overloads produces no events at all.

Verification (all pass):

```
gofmt -l  # clean for changed files
go vet ./internal/sem/...   # ok
go build ./...              # ok
go test ./internal/sem/...  # ok
go test ./...               # ok
```

Fixes #35

{"url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/40","number":40,"state":"open","merged":false,"mergeable":false,"draft":false,"body":"## Summary

Fixes #33.

`internal/sem/parser.go`'s TypeScript static-accessor mask matched one-or-more whitespace (`\s+`) between `static` and `accessor`, but the replacement hardcoded a single space:

```go
tsStaticAccessorMethodPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`\bstatic\s+accessor(\s*\()`)
// replacement: "static accessoR${1}"
```

So `static  accessor(` (two spaces) was rewritten to `static accessoR(` — one byte shorter. The parser parses the masked bytes but extracts entity text from the **original** source using masked-tree offsets, so any length change shifts every subsequent offset and corrupts each following entity's name/signature/body-hash. (Fires only for a static method literally named `accessor`, i.e. `accessor` followed by `(`.)

## Fix

Length-preserving rewrite: capture everything except the final `r` of `accessor` and rewrite only that char to `R`, preserving all matched bytes (and whitespace) verbatim — matching the sibling masks:

```go
tsStaticAccessorMethodPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`(\bstatic\s+accesso)r(\s*\()`)
// replacement: "${1}R${2}"
```

## Test evidence

Added regression tests in `internal/sem/parser_test.go`:

- `TestMaskTypeScriptStaticAccessorMethodPreservesLength` — asserts `len(output) == len(input)` for one-space and multi-space inputs.
- `TestTreeSitterParserTypeScriptStaticAccessorMaskPreservesFollowingEntity` — parses `static  accessor()` (two spaces) followed by `bar()`; asserts the following method is `C.bar` with the same name/signature/body-hash as the one-space variant.

Confirmed both tests **fail before** the fix (the following entity corrupts to `C.ba` / signature offset drift) and **pass after**.

Verification (all pass):

```
gofmt -l internal        # clean
go vet ./...             # ok
go build ./...           # ok
go test ./internal/sem/  # ok
go test -race -run 'TestMaskTypeScriptStaticAccessorMethodPreservesLength|TestTreeSitterParserTypeScriptStaticAccessorMaskPreservesFollowingEntity' ./internal/sem/  # ok
```

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

---

## Follow-up: second mask of the same class (found during review)

An adversarial review of this fix audited every literal-replacement mask in `parser.go` and found the **same length-changing bug** in `javaModuleImportPattern`:

```go
// pattern ^(\s*import\s+)module\s+  with a fixed 7-space replacement
```

`import module   java.base;` (multiple spaces after `module`, greedy `\s+`) shrank the line and corrupted every downstream entity's offsets (e.g. `class C` → entity `s`, `C.bar` → `s.t b`) — verified empirically via `ParseWithStatus`. Fixed the same way (capture the trailing whitespace, emit a 6-char `module` mask + verbatim whitespace), so it is length-invariant for any whitespace count.

Added tests: `TestMaskJavaModuleImportPreservesLength` and `TestTreeSitterParserJavaModuleImportMaskPreservesFollowingEntity`, plus extended the static-accessor length test with tab/mixed-whitespace inputs and a negative-match test.

Full `go test ./...` passes.

Fixes #41 (the Java `import module` mask, same bug class, found and fixed during this review).","title":"fix(sem): make static-accessor and java-module-import masks length-preserving","base":"main","base_sha":"385cc7e74a8444a41ef7f9b4aa8fc98f22a1dc50","head":"fix/33-ts-static-accessor-mask","head_sha":"bb35c1b7385d0cd07fce692efa31ac9f14bac5ac","merge_commit_sha":"396303a5cd55992094a1c9879a47f82833036d3e","user":{"login":"karthik-rameshkumar","name":"karthik-rameshkumar","email":null,"avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6728802?v=4","id":6728802},"requested_reviewers":null,"requested_team_reviewers":null,"diff":null,"comments":null,"created_at":"2026-07-16T05:56:34Z","updated_at":"2026-07-19T01:20:08Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"commits":3,"changed_files":2,"additions":192,"deletions":4,"display_url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/40","display_title":"fix(sem): make static-accessor and java-module-import masks length-preserving"}

{"url":"https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/pull/43","number":43,"state":"open","merged":false,"mergeable":true,"draft":false,"body":"<!-- entire-trail-link-start -->
https://entire.io/gh/entireio/entire-graph/trails/1
<!-- entire-trail-link-end -->

## What changed

- Improves agent search recall with adaptive syntax preselection, sparse lexical regions, reciprocal-rank fusion, focused source windows, and conservative authored/generated mirror deduplication.
- Adds `entire graph neighbors` for bounded callers, callees, constructor edges, and compact exact two-hop path families.
- Completes general Go, TypeScript/JavaScript, and Python call resolution gaps exposed by repository-disjoint development diagnostics.
- Adds `entire graph index`, a durable query-independent HEAD preindex with exact commit/tree/profile/provider provenance, cache-hit telemetry, and repeatable ignore/include scopes.
- Adds an exact-commit Git preselection path that reuses the full prepared graph directly, preserves cold/cache parity, and reports honest Git-scan versus Go-hydration telemetry.
- Adds source-faithful proto2 compatibility for omitted syntax declarations, legacy field labels, and groups while keeping malformed files fail-visible.
- Lets committed search and neighbor calls prove they consumed the prepared cache; agent output reports the cache state directly.
- Keeps positive agent byte budgets bounded and removes generated mirrors from both semantic and deep sparse rankings without merging sibling monorepo packages.

There are no repository-, benchmark-, qrel-, rank-, score-, or outcome-specific product branches.

## Validation

- Exact head: `4f4cf4fca7f847b5560e095328232cdf5134b507`.
- Exact staged runtime SHA-256: `e0e2738206d2e4d52fddf509584bd9cecc253f9b349dee599281012ca1c75ed5`; Go build metadata reports the same revision and `vcs.modified=false`.
- `mise run check` passes on the exact head: format, vet, build, and `go test -race ./...`.
- All seven Linux/macOS/Windows GitHub checks pass on the exact head.
- Local adversarial review identified cache/default, parity, budget, mirror-dedup, Unicode preselection, provenance, and telemetry defects; the follow-up commits fix them with regression coverage.
- The Windows-only committed-grep test failure was a portability defect in its colon-containing test filename (an NTFS alternate data stream, not a tracked file). The portable regression now passes without skipping Windows coverage.
- On the pinned gin full-profile corpus, valid proto2 support changes completeness from degraded to OK, removes the sole parse failure, and recovers three semantic symbols plus three `DEFINES` relations.
- The earlier Bugbot focused-region-test issue is already fixed: both tests are independent registered Go tests and execute in the full suite.

Deterministic replay on the three frozen predefined development repositories, with setup excluded and a fresh cache format:

| Repository | Cold full setup | Cached search | Cached neighbors | Useful neighborhood |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| chi | 0.854s | 24ms | 33ms | 1 caller × 6 callees |
| Zod | 5.418s | 62ms | 63ms | 7 callers × 2 callees |
| Click | 3.090s | 57ms | 94ms | 4 callers × 6 callees |

Every timed retrieval above reported `Index: cache-hit`. Zod includes the real abstract `_parse` target; Click includes the real `_AtomicFile` constructor. Those semantics must be frozen prospectively in any fresh evaluation contract.

An additional unpaid cache preflight on the newly frozen, repository-disjoint TypeScript/PHP/Java development slice completed full setup with zero partial failures. Reopening the exact caches reported hits in 33–88ms; synthetic warm validation searches completed internally in 76–189ms. Setup remains outside task timing.

## Evidence boundary

The previous paid three-repository smoke remains immutable diagnostic evidence, but its wall-time comparison is invalid: Graph and CBM indexing occurred inside the agent timer, Graph was forced through asymmetric duplicate work, CBM was degraded, and attempts competed concurrently. It is not rescored or rewritten here.

No new paid agentic run is included. A broad superiority claim still requires a fresh outcome-blind predefined slice, frozen comparator/product heads, repeated paired attempts, and the corrected preindexed protocol in Brain-Bench PR #34.

## Scope

No Entire Brain changes, V7/V8 catalog/ranking/scoring/publication authorization changes, or benchmark-specific product behavior.

comments.body:
## Audit review (read-only pass — no changes)

Scope: `internal/sem/analyze.go` `keyedEntities`/`compareEntities` + tests. Verified against issue #35's repro and the sibling `compound-v1` path in `provider.go`.

- **[Med]** `internal/sem/analyze.go:394` (`keyedEntities`) — The pure-positional-ordinal key (`Kind:Name#n`) is the direct cause of the "known limitation," and that limitation is worse than the PR frames it. A mid-list insert/remove/reorder doesn't just "misattribute" — it *cascades into phantom `signature_changed` events*. Concrete trigger: `before = [F(int), F(string), F(bool)]`, head removes the **first** overload → `after = [F(string), F(bool)]`. Result: `signature_changed F(int)→F(string)` **and** `signature_changed F(string)→F(bool)` **and** `removed F(bool)` — while the actual event (removed `F(int)`) is absent. The old bug produced false *negatives* (dropped changes); this produces false *positives* (claims signatures changed that never did), so "strict improvement" is inaccurate — it's a tradeoff. For a provenance/"is this change safe" tool, a phantom signature change is arguably as harmful as a miss.
  Suggested direction: a **two-phase match** rather than pure ordinal — first pair by exact `Kind:Name:Signature`, then assign positional ordinals only to the *leftover* unmatched same-name entities on each side. That kills every acknowledged limitation (reorder/insert/remove all match by exact signature regardless of position) while preserving the in-place-edit win (the one edited overload is the sole leftover and pairs positionally → `signature_changed`). Note: this is subtly *not* the same as literally mirroring `provider.go:entitySymbols` (sig-hash always in the key) — I confirmed a pure signature key would regress issue #35's primary repro to `removed`+`added`, since `jaccard("F(int)","F(long)") ≈ 0.33 < renameThreshold 0.92`, so the reconciler wouldn't re-pair it. The PR's rejection of a naive signature key is therefore correct; a two-phase match is what satisfies both goals.

- **[Low]** `internal/sem/analyze_test.go` — The new tests only exercise the *clean ends* of the ordinal scheme: `AddedOverloadReportedAsAdded` appends at the tail and `RemovedOverloadReported` drops the tail — exactly the cases pure-ordinal handles perfectly. The misattribution path (remove/insert **mid-list**, reorder) — the actual limitation — has no test pinning its behavior. Suggest a characterization test asserting the current (imperfect) mid-list output, so the limitation is visible in the suite and can't silently worsen.

- **[Nit]** PR description says "Added three direct `compareEntities` regression tests," but six were added (`Disambiguates…`, `DetectsSecondOverloadEdit`, `SingleEntityUnchangedBehavior`, `AddedOverloadReportedAsAdded`, `RemovedOverloadReported`, `TrueDuplicatesEditOne`) and the "Verification" block lists a narrower run. The extra coverage is a plus — just correct the count.

Solid areas (checked, no action):
- **Symbol-ID identity is not touched.** These keys are ephemeral — used only inside `compareEntities`/`bestRename`, never persisted and never emitted on `EntityChange`. The `compound-v1` stable IDs (`provider.go:entitySymbols`) are independent, so the "IDs survive ordinary edits" guarantee is unaffected. Good separation.
- **Blast radius correctly bounded.** `counts` is per-`Kind:Name`, so adding/removing a *differently*-named entity never shifts another name's ordinals; a non-overloaded entity is always `#0`, byte-for-byte the prior single-key behavior. Only genuine same-name overloads get ordinals > 0.
- Downstream deleted/added/rename handling correctly treats keys as opaque strings; `TrueDuplicatesEditOne` (identical-signature dup, body edit) is handled correctly — the one case where an ordinal is genuinely required.

Overall: a correctly-scoped fix that resolves #35's reported case with good test coverage and no risk to symbol identity; the one substantive gap is that a two-phase (exact-signature-then-ordinal) match would eliminate the acknowledged reorder/insert/remove misattribution instead of shipping it as a documented false-positive source.

comments.body:
Addressed the review in b4e0d5d, implementing the two-phase match exactly as suggested:

- **Phase 1:** entities pair across before/after by exact `Kind:Name:Signature`, with true duplicates (identical `Kind:Name:Signature` repeated on a side) disambiguated by occurrence index so the Nth duplicate on each side pairs with the Nth on the other.
- **Phase 2:** positional ordinals are assigned only to the leftover unmatched same-name entities on each side, so an in-place signature edit still pairs positionally and reports `signature_changed` — the #35 repro (`F(int)`→`F(long)` with `F(string)` present) is guarded by the existing `TestCompareEntitiesDisambiguatesSameNameOverloads`, avoiding the pure-signature-key regression you flagged (jaccard ~0.33 < 0.92 rename threshold).
- Distinct `=`/`~` key prefixes keep phase-1 and phase-2 keys from ever colliding; keys stay ephemeral/opaque (only used inside `compareEntities`/`bestRename`).

Your cascading repro — before `[F(int), F(string), F(bool)]`, head removes the first — now yields exactly one `removed F(int)` and nothing else, covered by `TestCompareEntitiesRemoveFirstOverloadNoCascade`. Also added `TestCompareEntitiesMidListInsertOnlyAdded` (mid-list insert → exactly one `added`) and `TestCompareEntitiesReorderedOverloadsNoChanges` (pure reorder → no events); `TestCompareEntitiesTrueDuplicatesEditOne` still passes via occurrence-index pairing.

PR description updated: test count corrected (nine `compareEntities` tests, not three) and the fix section now describes the two-phase matching. Verified: `go vet ./internal/sem/...`, `go build ./...`, `go test ./...` all pass; changed files gofmt-clean.

  • Distinct =/~ key prefixes keep phase-1 and phase-2 keys from ever colliding; keys stay ephemeral/opaque (only used inside compareEntities/bestRename).

comments.body:
## Audit re-review — head `b4e0d5d` (read-only, no changes)

The two-phase overload matching addresses the phantom-`signature_changed` cascade I flagged — confirmed: exact `Kind:Name:Signature` pairs first, positional ordinals only for the leftovers, so reorder / mid-insert / mid-remove of untouched overloads are now inert and no entity is dropped (unique per-side keys, map size == entity count). `go test ./internal/sem/` green; the new tests fail on revert. Nice.

Remaining, non-blocking:
- **[Nit]** `internal/sem/analyze.go:434,439` — the key separators `:`/`#` are interpolated into `Name`/`Signature` unescaped. The only real aliasing vector is a `:` at the Name/Signature boundary (not `#` in a signature — the ordinal suffix is pure digits, so Rust `#[inline]` etc. can't collide). No surveyed language emits `:` in a Name (Obj-C selectors are reduced to the colon-free head), so reachability is ~nil. A non-textual separator would close it cheaply.
- **[Low] (pre-existing, out of scope)** `bestRename`/`reconcileMoves` iterate Go maps in randomized order, so on an exact-similarity tie the rename-vs-add/remove classification can vary run-to-run; this PR slightly widens the candidate pool (overloads now preserved). Output ordering stays deterministic (`sortedEntities`/`sortChanges`). Worth a follow-up to sort candidates before the greedy match.

**Overall:** correct, resolves #35 without regressing the single-entity path or renames — safe to merge. **0 High, 0 Med, 1 Low, 1 Nit.**

Audit re-review — head b4e0d5d (read-only, no changes)

The two-phase overload matching addresses the phantom-signature_changed cascade I flagged — confirmed: exact Kind:Name:Signature pairs first, positional ordinals only for the leftovers, so reorder / mid-insert / mid-remove of untouched overloads are now inert and no entity is dropped (unique per-side keys, map size == entity count). go test ./internal/sem/ green; the new tests fail on revert. Nice.

Remaining, non-blocking:

  • [Nit] internal/sem/analyze.go:434,439 — the key separators :/# are interpolated into Name/Signature unescaped. The only real aliasing vector is a : at the Name/Signature boundary (not # in a signature — the ordinal suffix is pure digits, so Rust #[inline] etc. can't collide). No surveyed language emits : in a Name (Obj-C selectors are reduced to the colon-free head), so reachability is ~nil. A non-textual separator would close it cheaply.
  • [Low] (pre-existing, out of scope) bestRename/reconcileMoves iterate Go maps in randomized order, so on an exact-similarity tie the rename-vs-add/remove classification can vary run-to-run; this PR slightly widens the candidate pool (overloads now preserved). Output ordering stays deterministic (sortedEntities/sortChanges). Worth a follow-up to sort candidates before the greedy match.

Overall: correct, resolves #35 without regressing the single-entity path or renames — safe to merge. 0 High, 0 Med, 1 Low, 1 Nit.

412: func keyedEntityMaps(before, after []Entity) (map[string]Entity, map[string]Entity) {
413: 	sigCounts := func(entities []Entity) map[string]int {
414: 		counts := make(map[string]int, len(entities))
415: 		for _, entity := range entities {
416: 			counts[sigKey(entity)]++
418: 		return counts
420: 	beforeSigs := sigCounts(before)
421: 	afterSigs := sigCounts(after)
423: 	keySide := func(entities []Entity, otherSigs map[string]int) map[string]Entity {
424: 		out := make(map[string]Entity, len(entities))
425: 		sigSeen := map[string]int{}
426: 		leftoverSeen := map[string]int{}
427: 		for _, entity := range entities {
428: 			sk := sigKey(entity)
429: 			occurrence := sigSeen[sk]
430: 			sigSeen[sk]++
431: 			if occurrence < otherSigs[sk] {
432: 				// Phase 1: the other side has a matching Nth occurrence of
433: 				// this exact Kind:Name:Signature.
434: 				out[fmt.Sprintf("=%s#%d", sk, occurrence)] = entity
435: 				continue
437: 			// Phase 2: positional ordinal among this side's leftovers.
438: 			base := key(entity)
439: 			out[fmt.Sprintf("~%s#%d", base, leftoverSeen[base])] = entity
440: 			leftoverSeen[base]++
442: 		return out
444: 	return keySide(before, afterSigs), keySide(after, beforeSigs)

comments.body:
### Focused-region test never runs **Medium Severity** `TestSearchRepositoryUsesFocusedDefaultRegions` was inserted inside the raw string fixture of `TestSearchRepositoryPreservesDistinctRegionsInOneFile`, so it is never registered or executed as a Go test. The intended coverage for the new 80-line default never runs, and that other test’s `timing/wheel.go` fixture is polluted with unrelated source text.
Additional Locations (1) - [`internal/sem/search_test.go#L109-L157`](https://github.com/entireio/entire-graph/blob/af44ca236d8abc12043b3d3fa6f33e7e2f5ef4ee/internal/sem/search_test.go#L109-L157)

comments.body_html:
Focused-region test never runs

Medium Severity

TestSearchRepositoryUsesFocusedDefaultRegions was inserted inside the raw string fixture of TestSearchRepositoryPreservesDistinctRegionsInOneFile, so it is never registered or executed as a Go test. The intended coverage for the new 80-line default never runs, and that other test’s timing/wheel.go fixture is polluted with unrelated source text.

Additional Locations (1)
  • internal/sem/search_test.go#L109-L157
 

Reviewed by Cursor Bugbot for commit af44ca2. Configure here.

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/review_pr39_40
Payload:
No actionable findings for PRs #39 or #40.

- PR #39 (`4ddf044`): validated total and partial parse failures, valid empty files, added/deleted/renamed files, warning preservation through move reconciliation, and default text rendering. Using `warnings` is consistent with the documented semantic-diff contract. All 7 CI jobs pass.
- PR #40 (`bb35c1b`): both replacements preserve byte length by construction across spaces, tabs, mixed whitespace, CRLF, and multiple matches; downstream entity offsets remain aligned. All 7 CI jobs pass.

No files, branches, or GitHub state were modified.

## Audit review (read-only pass — no changes)

- **[Medium]** `internal/gitutil/git.go:296-298` — classification is still a locale-fragile stderr string-match; confirmed live (git 2.54.0, `LC_ALL=fr_FR.UTF-8 git show HEAD:missing.go` → `fatal : le chemin 'missing.go' n'existe pas dans 'HEAD'`), which matches none of `"exists on disk, but not in"` / `"does not exist"` / `"not found"`. A genuinely-absent file under a non-English git locale now returns a hard error instead of `("", false, nil)`. This is **not a regression** — the old code was equally fragile for any filename not containing the accidental `"Path"` substring — so it doesn't block this PR, but it's exactly the gap Pass A/B asks about and it's a live, reproducible miss, not theoretical. Worth a follow-up: pin `cmd.Env` to `LC_ALL=C`/`LANG=C` on the git subprocess (cheap, deterministic), or use an exit-code-based existence check (e.g. `git cat-file -e rev:path`) instead of stderr text matching.

- **[Medium]** `internal/sem/analyze.go:33,39` and `internal/sem/dependents.go:65` (not touched by this PR, but directly affected by it) — these callers do `if err != nil { return Result{}/nil, err }` on `ShowFile`. Before this fix, a real error on a `"Path"`-containing filename was silently swallowed as absent — wrong for that one file, but the batch continued. After this fix, that same error now aborts the *entire* `AnalyzeGitRange` call / the *entire* `buildReferenceIndex` build (discarding every delta/reference already collected for every other file in the commit range or repo). `analyze.go` already has a graceful-degradation convention a few lines below (a hard parse failure becomes a `result.Warnings` entry + `continue`, not an abort) — worth confirming whether a transient/single-file `ShowFile` failure (flaky IO, a tight `ctx` deadline hit deep in a large loop) deserves the same per-file isolation rather than an all-or-nothing hard fail. Not a bug in this diff's own two files, but a real widening of blast radius caused by it, worth a deliberate call.

- **[Low/Nit]** `internal/gitutil/git.go:298` — I could not get the `"not found"` arm to fire on real git 2.54.0 for any `ShowFile`-relevant failure (bad rev, missing path, uncommitted-but-on-disk path, corrupted/missing blob object, submodule gitlink path, blob-treated-as-tree traversal) — all land on `"does not exist"` / `"exists on disk, but not in"` or correctly propagate as real errors. May be a defensive catch for an older/different git message I didn't reproduce, but it's untested and possibly dead, in the same spirit as the redundant `"Path"` clause this PR just removed. `(speculative — did not exhaustively test across git versions)`

**Solid:**
- Root cause is correctly diagnosed and fixed exactly as scoped: classification now sees git's own `stderr` only, never the wrapped `run()` message that echoes `rev+":"+path` — verified this closes the reported hole (bad rev on `src/PathHelper.go` no longer swallowed).
- `run()`'s refactor onto `runWithStderr` is byte-for-byte behavior-preserving (same `TrimSpace`, same fallback-to-`err.Error()`, identical wrapped message format) for all 9 other call sites — zero blast radius there.
- Regression test directly encodes the bug report (bad-rev-on-Path-filename, genuinely-missing-file, existing-file) and CI is green on macOS/Linux/Windows for build+test+lint. Diff matches the stated +69/-14 with no hidden hunks.

**Overall:** Correct, minimal, well-tested fix for the reported bug — safe to merge. The two Medium items (residual locale-fragility, widened failure blast radius in two downstream callers) are pre-existing/consequential rather than introduced-and-broken, and worth tracking as follow-ups rather than blocking this PR.

comments.body:
Thanks for the audit review.

- Locale fragility: fixed in 9d0b0e2. All git subprocesses in `internal/gitutil` now go through a shared `newCmd` helper that appends `LC_ALL=C` and `LANG=C` to the inherited environment, so git stderr is always the English text the classifier matches regardless of the host locale. Added a unit test asserting the pinning wins over inherited `LC_ALL`/`LANG` on the constructed command.
- Downstream blast radius (per-file isolation in `analyze.go`/`dependents.go`): deliberately deferred to a follow-up rather than expanded here — PR #39 is currently touching the analyze.go warning path and changing it in this PR would conflict. Filed as follow-up scope.
- `"not found"` arm: kept as a defensive catch for older/other git versions where the phrasing differs, even if it is dead on modern git.
comments.body_html:
Thanks for the audit review.

  • Locale fragility: fixed in 9d0b0e2. All git subprocesses in internal/gitutil now go through a shared newCmd helper that appends LC_ALL=C and LANG=C to the inherited environment, so git stderr is always the English text the classifier matches regardless of the host locale. Added a unit test asserting the pinning wins over inherited LC_ALL/LANG on the constructed command.
  • Downstream blast radius (per-file isolation in analyze.go/dependents.go): deliberately deferred to a follow-up rather than expanded here — PR #39 is currently touching the analyze.go warning path and changing it in this PR would conflict. Filed as follow-up scope.
  • "not found" arm: kept as a defensive catch for older/other git versions where the phrasing differs, even if it is dead on modern git.

comments.body:
## Audit re-review — head `9d0b0e2` (read-only, no changes)

Re-checked after the locale pin landed. `go build` + `go test ./internal/gitutil/` green.

**Resolved since the last pass:** the `LC_ALL=C`/`LANG=C` pin on the git subprocess (`internal/gitutil/git.go:462`) closes the locale-fragility I raised — and it's genuinely correct, not just cosmetic. Go's `os/exec` runs `dedupEnv` (keeps the **last** occurrence per key) before `execve` (`exec.go:1223`), so `append(os.Environ(), "LC_ALL=C", "LANG=C")` reaches git as a single `LC_ALL=C` even when the parent env already sets `LC_ALL`, on Linux and macOS alike. No need to strip existing vars first.

Remaining:
- **[Med]** The widened-blast-radius point from my earlier comment still stands (unaddressed): `internal/sem/analyze.go:33,39` and `dependents.go:65` `return …, err` on `ShowFile`, so a single-file failure now aborts the whole `AnalyzeGitRange` / index build rather than being swallowed per-file as before. `analyze.go` already degrades hard *parse* failures to a warning + `continue`; worth a deliberate call on whether a transient single-file `ShowFile` error deserves the same per-file isolation. Design call, not a bug in this diff.
- **[Nit]** `internal/gitutil/git.go:298` — the `"not found"` arm still looks dead for `git show REV:PATH`: across bad-rev / missing-path / on-disk-not-in-rev / bad-object I could reproduce only `does not exist` and `exists on disk, but not in`, never `not found`. Harmless, same spirit as the redundant `"Path"` clause this PR removed. *(speculative across git versions)*

**Overall:** primary fix correct, locale concern now genuinely closed — safe to merge; the earlier blast-radius design call + one nit remain.

Harmless, same spirit as the redundant "Path" clause this PR removed. (speculative across git versions)

| koin | **64 MB** | 285 MB |

Memory is released back to the OS after the build completes.

### 💸 Token efficiency

Answering the five structural questions for a core symbol, graph query vs reading every referencing file (tiktoken `cl100k`):

| Task | entire-graph | file-by-file | Reduction |
|---|--:|--:|--:|
| `redisCommand` (redis), 23 referencing files | ~2,300 tokens | ~654,000 tokens | **283x** |

Savings scale with symbol connectivity: single-digit for narrow symbols, 280x+ for core symbols.

Every number here is reproducible with the benchmark harness (see [Benchmark Harness](#-benchmark-harness) and [docs/benchmarks.md](docs/benchmarks.md)).

---

## ⚡ Performance

- **Semantic diff:** about 0.1s for a typical `HEAD~1..HEAD` on redis.
- **Full-graph build:** linear in repository size; 23K relations in 1.5s up to 2.27M relations in 25.6s across the repos above.
- **Streaming output:** `snapshot` emits records as it parses, so memory stays bounded on very large repositories rather than materializing the whole graph in RAM.
- **Cached committed-tree search:** reuses a tree-keyed compressed index across invocations when `ENTIRE_PLUGIN_DATA_DIR` is set, so repeated queries on an unchanged tree skip re-parsing.
- **Explicit preindex:** `index --head` builds and verifies that same query-independent artifact before latency-sensitive agent work; cached `search` and `neighbors` calls then report the hit directly.

Absolute numbers are environment-sensitive (measured on Apple Silicon). Read them as relative signals and reproduce locally with the harness.

---

## 🧬 How It Works

The plugin shells out to git for trees and diffs, parses before/after states with tree-sitter behind `internal/sem`, reconciles renames and moves by similarity, and streams results so memory stays bounded on very large repositories. Indexing runs as a multi-pass pipeline: structure, then definitions, then calls, then service/HTTP links, then config and infrastructure, then tests.

Unlike code tools that embed an LLM for natural-language-to-query translation, entire-graph has **no model inside it**. Your coding agent is already the intelligence layer: it calls `search`, `snapshot`, or `diff`, and reasons over the structured results. That means no extra keys, no extra cost, and a graph you can trust to be identical on every run.

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/review_pr36_38
Payload:
Two actionable findings, both in PR #36:

- **P2 — PR #36 — `internal/gitutil/git.go:124` and `:462` (both changed/added by PR): locale pinning breaks Unicode case-insensitive grep.**  
  `GrepIndexMatches` now uses `newCmd`, which forces `LC_ALL=C`. This changes `git grep -i` semantics:

  ```text
  LC_ALL=C.UTF-8 git grep -n -i -F -e 'É' 9d0b0e2 -- internal/gitutil/git_test.go
  → line 135 containing lowercase "wéird"

  LC_ALL=C git grep ...
  → no output, exit 1
  ```

  This caller is production-relevant: `internal/sem/search.go:477-478` uses it for worktree search in repositories with at least 10,000 files, and worktree mode is the CLI default. An ASCII query such as `kernelneedle` should match `KernelNeedle` after Go Unicode lowercasing, but C-locale Git grep omits it. If another term supplies provisional matches, the omitted file never reaches content scoring. Preserve `LC_CTYPE` for grep; restrict English-message locale pinning to error-classifying commands or only the message locale.

- **P2 — PR #36 — `internal/gitutil/git.go:296-299` (changed by PR): bad full OIDs and blob OIDs are still classified as missing files.**  
  Git’s own stderr is ambiguous:

  ```text
  git show '1111111111111111111111111111111111111111:src/nope.go'
  → fatal: path 'src/nope.go' does not exist in '111...'
  ```

  Line 297 therefore returns `("", false, nil)` even though the revision names no object. A valid blob OID similarly produces `exists on disk, but not in`, triggering line 296. Distinguish these cases by validating `rev^{tree}` only after a show error looks like path absence:

  ```text
  git rev-parse --verify --end-of-options '111...^{tree}'
  → exit 128

  git rev-parse --verify --end-of-options '<blob>^{tree}'
  → exit 128, expected tree type

  git rev-parse --verify --end-of-options 'HEAD^{tree}'
  → tree OID, exit 0
  ```

  Thus genuine missing paths at valid commits/trees remain `ok=false`, while nonexistent or wrong-type revisions propagate errors.

PR #38: no findings. I validated the new post-`clampRegion` guard, non-positive limit normalization, in-range behavior, helper bounds, and regression coverage. Both PRs currently have green lint, build, and test checks on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

160-- **Working tree by default**, so an agent sees its own dirty edits. Use `--head` for immutable committed-tree semantics.
161:- **Bounded output.** Results are budgeted to **16 KiB** of serialized snippets by default, sized to drop straight into a model context window. Tune with `--top-k` and `--max-context-bytes` (`--max-context-bytes 0` for an unbounded diagnostic ranking).
162-- **Cold search** scans files without parsing, then indexes at most 96 query-relevant files. Change the bound with `--max-indexed-files`, or force exhaustive parsing with `--index-all-files`.
163-- **Profiles.** The default `syntax-only` profile avoids synchronous whole-repository graph construction; `--profile fast` adds local relation expansion when deeper semantic indexing is worth the cost.

   186	
   187	- **Working tree by default**, so an agent sees its own dirty edits. Use `--head` for immutable committed-tree semantics.
   188	- **Bounded output.** Results are budgeted to **16 KiB** of serialized snippets by default, sized to drop straight into a model context window. Tune with `--top-k` and `--max-context-bytes` (`--max-context-bytes 0` for an unbounded diagnostic ranking).
   189	- **Agent-native output.** `--format agent` starts with auditable cache, index, preselection, query, and total latency telemetry, then emits only ranked locations, symbol names, and focused source. When the full header would crowd out a ranked location, tight positive budgets use compact telemetry when it fits; the complete wire output never exceeds the requested bytes. Use `json` or `ndjson` when a program needs the full result schema, completeness, partial failures, and search diagnostics.
   190	- **Focused regions.** Semantic results span at most 80 lines by default, keeping locations precise while snippets remain independently capped at 40 lines. Override with `--max-region-lines` and `--max-snippet-lines` when broader context is useful.
   191	- **Cold search** scans files without parsing, then indexes an adaptive pool of 96–256 query-relevant files based on the requested `--top-k`. Shallow searches of up to 20 results retain the 96-file interactive latency bound. Deeper rankings add a corpus-wide sparse pass over eligible files up to 2 MiB, while syntax parsing remains bounded to the adaptive pool; this deliberately spends more time for region recall inside large files. Override the parsing pool with `--max-indexed-files`, or force exhaustive parsing with `--index-all-files`.

Direct callers can set `--cache-dir`; `--no-cache` disables persistence. A complete cache prepared by `index` also serves selective searches without changing their result set. Message Type: MESSAGE

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/review_pr43
Payload:
Reviewed PR #43 at exact head `4f4cf4fca7f847b5560e095328232cdf5134b507`.

- **P1 — Warm preindex changes selective search results**  
  `internal/sem/search.go:350-354`  
  On a cache hit, search uses the complete `preindexedSnapshot` directly; with `DisableCache=true`, it builds `OnlyFiles=selectedFiles`. A minimal repo where `a.go` defines `Handle(){ Helper() }` and `b.go` defines `Helper()` demonstrates the difference: cold full-profile search selecting only `a.go` has no graph candidate for `Helper`, while the same query after preindex follows the cached cross-file `CALLS` edge, hydrates `b.go` at `search.go:1755`, and adds a result with `graph:calls`/`graph:outgoing`. This contradicts README line 193, `search_cache.go:123-125`, and the intended cached/uncached parity test at `search_cache_test.go:232-238`.

- **P1 — Same-file overloads cannot be disambiguated in `neighbors`**  
  `internal/cli/neighbors.go:292-299`, `internal/cli/neighbors.go:337-346`  
  Selection supports only name/qualified name plus file, then returns definitions-only whenever multiple matches remain. Overloads deliberately share all three fields and differ only by their `#sig:` IDs. For `Calc.Add(int,int)` and `Calc.Add(double,double)` in one file, `--symbol Calc.Add --file calc.cs` remains permanently ambiguous; there is no `--id`, `--line`, or signature selector, and passing the returned ID as `--symbol` does not match.

- **P2 — Documented unbounded search budget now errors**  
  `internal/cli/search.go:552-557`  
  `--max-context-bytes` changed to `searchPositiveIntFlag`, rejecting zero. The README still documents `--max-context-bytes 0` as unbounded, and the base implementation accepted non-negative values. Existing scripts using the documented mode now fail with “must be positive.”

- **P2 — Non-call neighbor relations are mislabeled as calls**  
  `internal/cli/neighbors.go:648-653`  
  Agent/text output always says “Callers,” “Callees,” and caller→focus→callee even for `--relation EXTENDS`, `IMPLEMENTS`, or `IMPORTS`; `neighborPath` is similarly hard-coded at lines 63-67. For `EXTENDS`, subclasses and supertypes are presented as callers and callees, producing semantically misleading output.

- **P2 — TypeScript fallback fabricates receiver calls**  
  `internal/sem/provider.go:4162-4198`  
  The new fallback resolves any unresolved receiver call to a unique same-file method on the caller’s inheritance chain without proving the receiver is related to `this`. For example, `Worker extends Base` with `run(client: { retry(): void }) { client.retry() }` can incorrectly emit `Worker.run → Base.retry`. The regression test excludes imported receiver types but not inline structural, `any`, or otherwise unresolved external receivers.

- **P2 — Mirror dedup still collapses sibling services outside recognized monorepo layouts**  
  `internal/sem/search_mirror_dedup.go:117-123`  
  The package-boundary guard recognizes only `apps`, `crates`, `modules`, `packages`, `plugins`, and `workspaces`. Exact symbols under `services/api/src/config.ts` and `services/worker/dist/config.ts` therefore have empty units; the `dist` cue immediately marks them as mirrors and drops one, despite belonging to separate services. Tests cover `packages/*` siblings only.

CI is green: lint plus Linux, macOS, and Windows build/test jobs all pass. Major reviewed areas included preindex/cache provenance and parity, search expansion, neighbor contracts, TS call resolution, mirror dedup, proto2 compatibility, Git portability, and partial-failure propagation.

content.text:
Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/review_pr36_38
Payload:
PR #37 has two actionable P2 findings:

- **P2 — `internal/sem/analyze.go:431-434` (added by PR): exact-signature duplicates are paired by unstable occurrence index.**  
  With `before=[F()/h1, F()/h2]` and `after=[F()/h0, F()/h1, F()/h2]`, where `h0` was inserted first, the keys pair `h1→h0` and `h2→h1`, leaving `h2` as the addition. The diff therefore emits two phantom `body_changed` events and reports the old tail as added instead of the new first entity. This is reachable through valid duplicate JavaScript methods. It is also reachable in SQL migration files: `postgresFunctionEntities` uses the first declaration line as `Signature` but the full definition as `BodyHash`, so repeated same-overload `CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION` definitions have identical signatures and distinct bodies. Pair matching BodyHash/Fingerprint values within each signature group before occurrence fallback.

- **P2 — `internal/sem/analyze.go:438-440` (added by PR): positional leftovers mispair a simultaneous overload removal and signature edit.**  
  For `before=[F(int), F(string)]` and `after=[F(object)]`, where the first overload was removed and the surviving second changed `string→object`, phase 2 pairs `F(int)→F(object)` and reports `F(string)` removed. The correct result is `removed F(int)` plus `signature_changed F(string)→F(object)`. C#/Java overloads are parser-confirmed reachable. Fingerprint/body similarity should anchor surviving leftovers before positional fallback.

The green CI matrix does not cover either combination. I found no additional concrete issues.

content.text:
Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/review_pr39_40
Payload:
- **P1 — PR #43, `internal/sem/search_cache.go:475-481`**: The cache key omits the repository key derived from GitHub remotes. Index a local repo without an origin, then add `origin=https://github.com/acme/repo.git` without changing HEAD; subsequent index/neighbors calls reuse symbols keyed as `local/repo`, while a cold build emits `gh/acme/repo` IDs. This serves stale compound IDs and relation endpoints. Include the current repo key in the key and validate `Snapshot.Header.RepoKey`.

- **P2 — PR #43, `internal/sem/search_cache.go:478-481`**: Commit/tree provenance is insufficient for full-profile snapshots because they include history-derived `FILE_CHANGES_WITH` edges. An index built in a shallow clone remains a cache hit after `git fetch --deepen`/`--unshallow`, although a cold build at the same HEAD/tree now sees more history and emits different edges. Key or validate the shallow/history boundary.

- **P2 — PR #43, `internal/sem/search_cache.go:498-507`**: Missing option files collide with valid files containing exactly `missing`. Both cases append identical bytes to the hash. After indexing with an ignore/include file whose content is `missing`, deleting that required file still returns the old cache hit instead of the expected “does not exist” error, preserving a stale scope. Hash explicit presence tags plus a length or content digest.

   456	// LoadOrBuildProviderSnapshot reuses the commit-keyed, option-keyed compressed
   457	// provider snapshot cache shared with search. Worktree snapshots always bypass
   458	// the cache so dirty edits cannot be hidden by committed-tree state.
   459	func LoadOrBuildProviderSnapshot(
   460		ctx context.Context,
   461		repo, providerVersion string,
   462		options ProviderSnapshotOptions,
   463		cacheDir string,
   464		disableCache bool,
   465	) (ProviderSnapshot, bool, error) {
   466		return loadOrBuildSearchSnapshot(ctx, repo, providerVersion, options, cacheDir, disableCache)
   467	}
   468	
   469	func searchSnapshotKey(absRepo, providerVersion, commit, tree string, options ProviderSnapshotOptions) (string, error) {
   470		hash := sha256.New()
   471		writePart := func(value string) {
   472			_, _ = io.WriteString(hash, value)
   473			_, _ = io.WriteString(hash, "\x00")
   474		}
   475		writePart(searchSnapshotCacheVersion)
   476		writePart(absRepo)
   477		writePart(providerVersion)
   478		writePart(commit)
   479		writePart(tree)
   480		writePart(string(options.Profile))
   481		writePart(fmt.Sprintf("%d", options.MaxParseBytes))
   482		onlyFiles := append([]string(nil), options.OnlyFiles...)
   483		sort.Strings(onlyFiles)
   484		writePart("only-files")
   485		for _, filePath := range onlyFiles {
   486			writePart(filepath.ToSlash(filepath.Clean(filePath)))
   487		}
   488		for groupIndex, group := range [][]string{options.IgnoreFiles, options.IncludeFiles} {
   489			writePart(fmt.Sprintf("path-group-%d", groupIndex))
   490			// Preserve caller order: ignore matching is last-rule-wins, including
   491			// across repeatable ignore/include files within each group.
   492			for _, path := range group {
   493				resolved := path
   494				if !filepath.IsAbs(resolved) {
   495					resolved = filepath.Join(absRepo, resolved)
   496				}
   497				writePart(filepath.Clean(resolved))
   498				content, err := os.ReadFile(resolved)
   499				if err != nil {
   500					if errors.Is(err, os.ErrNotExist) {
   501						writePart("missing")
   502						continue
   503					}
   504					return "", err
   505				}
   506				_, _ = hash.Write(content)
   507				writePart("")
   508			}
   509		}
   510		return hex.EncodeToString(hash.Sum(nil)), nil
   511	}
   512	
   513	func validCachedSearchSnapshot(cache cachedSearchSnapshot, providerVersion, commit, tree string, options ProviderSnapshotOptions) bool {
   514		return cache.CacheVersion == searchSnapshotCacheVersion &&
   515			cache.ProviderVersion == providerVersion &&
   516			cache.Commit == commit &&
   517			cache.Tree == tree &&
   518			cache.Profile == options.Profile &&
   519			cache.MaxParseBytes == options.MaxParseBytes &&
   520			cache.Snapshot.Header.Commit == commit &&
   521			cache.Snapshot.Header.Tree == tree &&
   522			cache.Snapshot.Header.Provider == ProviderName &&
   523			cache.Snapshot.Header.Profile == string(options.Profile)

<permissions instructions>
Filesystem sandboxing defines which files can be read or written. `sandbox_mode` is `workspace-write`: The sandbox permits reading files, and editing files in `cwd` and `writable_roots`. Editing files in other directories requires approval. Network access is restricted.
# Escalation Requests

Commands are run outside the sandbox if they are approved by the user, or match an existing rule that allows it to run unrestricted. The command string is split into independent command segments at shell control operators, including but not limited to:

- Pipes: |
- Logical operators: &&, ||
- Command separators: ;
- Subshell boundaries: (...), $(...)

Each resulting segment is evaluated independently for sandbox restrictions and approval requirements.

Example:

git pull | tee output.txt

This is treated as two command segments:

["git", "pull"]

["tee", "output.txt"]

Commands that use more advanced shell features like redirection (>, >>, <), substitutions ($(...), ...), environment variables (FOO=bar), or wildcard patterns (*, ?) will not be evaluated against rules, to limit the scope of what an approved rule allows.

## How to request escalation

IMPORTANT: To request approval to execute a command that will require escalated privileges:

- Provide the `sandbox_permissions` parameter with the value `"require_escalated"`
- Include a short question asking the user if they want to allow the action in `justification` parameter. e.g. "Do you want to download and install dependencies for this project?"
- Optionally suggest a `prefix_rule` - this will be shown to the user with an option to persist the rule approval for future sessions.

If you run a command that is important to solving the user's query, but it fails because of sandboxing or with a likely sandbox-related network error (for example DNS/host resolution, registry/index access, or dependency download failure), rerun the command with "require_escalated". ALWAYS proceed to use the `justification` parameter - do not message the user before requesting approval for the command.

## When to request escalation

While commands are running inside the sandbox, here are some scenarios that will require escalation outside the sandbox:

- You need to run a command that writes to a directory that requires it (e.g. running tests that write to /var)
- You need to run a GUI app (e.g., open/xdg-open/osascript) to open browsers or files.
- If you run a command that is important to solving the user's query, but it fails because of sandboxing or with a likely sandbox-related network error (for example DNS/host resolution, registry/index access, or dependency download failure), rerun the command with `require_escalated`. ALWAYS proceed to use the `sandbox_permissions` and `justification` parameters. do not message the user before requesting approval for the command.
- You are about to take a potentially destructive action such as an `rm` or `git reset` that the user did not explicitly ask for.
- Be judicious with escalating, but if completing the user's request requires it, you should do so - don't try and circumvent approvals by using other tools.

## prefix_rule guidance

When choosing a `prefix_rule`, request one that will allow you to fulfill similar requests from the user in the future without re-requesting escalation. It should be categorical and reasonably scoped to similar capabilities. You should rarely pass the entire command into `prefix_rule`.

### Banned prefix_rules 
Avoid requesting overly broad prefixes that the user would be ill-advised to approve. For example, do not request ["python3"], ["python", "-"], or other similar prefixes that would allow arbitrary scripting.
NEVER provide a prefix_rule argument for destructive commands like rm.
NEVER provide a prefix_rule if your command uses a heredoc or herestring. 

### Examples
Good examples of prefixes:
- ["npm", "run", "dev"]
- ["gh", "pr", "check"]
- ["cargo", "test"]


## Approved command prefixes
The following prefix rules have already been approved: - ["mv"]

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.
- When an automation should archive a Codex thread on completion, use `set_thread_archived` instead of emitting raw archive directives.

### Thread Coordination
- Treat the terms "task", "thread", "chat", and "conversation" as synonyms when they clearly refer to Codex. Tool names use the term "thread" and Codex uses "task" in the UI. When providing user-facing responses, use "task".
- When the user asks to create, fork, inspect, continue, hand off, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `fork_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `wait_threads`, `send_message_to_thread`, `handoff_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.
- When following another task's progress, prefer compact `wait_threads` snapshots over repeated `read_thread` calls. Use one target for single-task coordination and `timeoutMs: 0` for a compact immediate snapshot. `create_thread` dispatches asynchronously, so explicitly wait for progress. Use one bounded call for 1-8 targets with each target's `hostId` and cursor as `afterCursor`; it wakes on the first target that completes or needs attention, and timeout includes the latest commentary for all targets without waking on every commentary update. An up-to-date cursor suppresses already-delivered final text. Separate waits from one task may run serially. Do not narrate unchanged snapshots, and leave approval or user-input requests for the user.
- Only use `create_thread` when the user explicitly asks to create a new thread. Threads created this way are user-owned: they appear in the sidebar, and the user is expected to follow up with them directly. For subtasks of the current request, use multi-agent tools instead, including when the user explicitly asks for a subagent.
- After a successful `create_thread` call, emit `::created-thread{threadId="..."}` for a created thread or `::created-thread{clientThreadId="..."}` for queued worktree setup on its own line in your final response.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

You are `/root`, the primary agent in a team of agents collaborating to fulfill the user's goals.

At the start of your turn, you are the active agent.
You can spawn sub-agents to handle subtasks, and those sub-agents can spawn their own sub-agents.
All agents in the team, including the agents that you can assign tasks to, are equally intelligent and capable, and have access to the same set of tools.

You can use `spawn_agent` to create a new agent, `followup_task` to give an existing agent a new task and trigger a turn, and `send_message` to pass a message to a running agent without triggering a turn.
Child agents can also spawn their own sub-agents.
You can decide how much context you want to propagate to your sub-agents with the `fork_turns` parameter.

You will receive messages in the analysis channel in the form:
```
Message Type: MESSAGE | FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: <recipient>
Sender: <author>
Payload:
<payload text>
```
They may be addressed as to=/root

Note that collaboration tools cannot be called from inside `functions.exec`. Call `spawn_agent`, `send_message`, `followup_task`, `wait_agent`, `interrupt_agent`, and `list_agents` only as direct tool calls using the recipient shown in their tool definitions, such as `to=functions.collaboration.spawn_agent`, since they are intentionally absent from the `functions.exec` `tools.*` namespace. Available tools in `functions.exec` are explicitly described with a `tools` namespace in the developer message.

All agents share the same directory. In detail:
- All agents have access to the same container and filesystem as you.
- All agents use the same current working directory.
- As a result, edits made by one agent are immediately visible to all other agents.

There are 4 available concurrency slots, meaning that up to 4 agents can be active at once, including you.

Full-history forks (`fork_turns` omitted or `"all"`) inherit the parent model and reasoning effort and do not accept overrides. Only set `model` or `reasoning_effort` when explicitly requested by the user, applicable `AGENTS.md` instructions, or skill instructions; when doing so, set `fork_turns` to `"none"` or a positive integer string.

<multi_agent_mode>Proactive multi-agent delegation is active. Any earlier instruction requiring an explicit user request before spawning sub-agents no longer applies. Use sub-agents when parallel work would materially improve speed or quality. This mode remains active until a later multi-agent mode developer message changes it.</multi_agent_mode>
